Creative Your Burnout Morning Poster (Customized to Your Personality & Aesthetic) Created using @AdobeFirefly Partnered Model Prompt - Use my uploaded face as the ONLY facial reference. Preserve my exact identity, facial structure, expressive eyes, skin tone, lips, nose shape, and recognizable features with zero facial drift. Create an ultra-detailed cinematic 3D chibi-style portrait inspired by premium Pixar and modern collectible character art. The character should represent an exhausted but genius-level AI creator girl after an all-night creative session. Scene: She is sitting sleepily on the edge of a rustic wooden table early in the morning, looking emotionally drained but unintentionally adorable. Messy fluffy hair tied loosely in a chaotic bun with strands falling everywhere, sleepy half-open eyes, soft pout, slightly red nose, tired “I edited all night” expression. She wears oversized pastel sleepwear with subtle creative-tech aesthetics: soft ivory pajama set tiny “Future Vibes AI” embroidery cozy cardigan slipping off one shoulder fluffy duck slippers delicate pearl earrings Environment: Warm moody morning atmosphere with soft cinematic lighting, steam rising from a cup of coffee beside her, blurred artistic studio background, creative workspace energy. Around the character include subtle hints of her personality: tiny floating holographic video timelines miniature film clapperboard AI art sketches tiny glowing travel icons scattered sticky notes with creative ideas small laptop showing unfinished edits scribbled concepts YouTube play icon soft pastel doodles Expression & Mood: She should look like: an overthinking creative genius emotionally exhausted but lovable sleep-deprived filmmaker energy “just one more edit” vibe quiet chaos mixed with soft humor Style: Ultra-premium stylized realism, collectible figurine quality, ultra-detailed hair strands, realistic fabric texture, soft skin shading, cinematic depth of field, dreamy muted pastel tones, cozy Pinterest aesthetic, subtle humor, emotional storytelling. Lighting: Soft window light, warm cinematic shadows, gentle volumetric haze, realistic steam glow from coffee. Color Palette: Mocha brown, warm cream, dusty peach, muted teal, ivory, soft caramel, pastel gold accents. Composition: Centered symmetrical composition, shallow depth of field, highly expressive face, clean luxury aesthetic, social-media-ready, 8K ultra detailed. Add tiny handwritten doodles around the scene: “rendering…” “creative chaos” “future vibes” “1% battery” “one more export” Negative Prompt: bad anatomy, distorted face, extra fingers, plastic skin, overexposed highlights, blurry eyes, cartoonish proportions, low detail, bad hands, uncanny face, oversaturated colors, duplicate objects, warped furniture, messy composition, low resolution.

This is the future of coding agents: specialized, high-speed subagents handling critical workflows in the background. Proud to see @augmentcode running Mercury 2 in production for context compaction with major latency and cost improvements.

@augmentcode rebuilt their context compaction layer around Mercury 2. 82% latency cut. 90% cost cut. Comparable quality to Opus 4.7. Running in production today. "We took a counter-intuitive bet. We decoupled summarization entirely, offloading it to Mercury 2 as a dedicated subagent. Mercury 2 is the highly efficient engine powering our most critical workflows." -@RustagiAnkur & @jm1234567890, Members of Technical Staff at Augment Code The subagent layer needs the most efficient model. Full methodology and eval setup in the writeup. https://t.co/LPVTdaMjli

AI-generated software is becoming its own software supply chain. @cursor_ai, @Lovable, @Replit, @boltdotnew,@claudeai, @grok, agents, dApps, MCP tools, all of them are moving code from prompt to production faster than traditional security workflows can keep up. That creates a new requirement: security validation has to become model-native. Nullsec-S1 is being built for that layer. A security-specialized LLM that sits between AI-generated code and production, analyzing auth logic, secrets, env exposure, SQLi, SSRF, XSS, MCP/tool abuse, shell execution, dependency risk, wallet flows, and safe-code calibration. AI builds the software. Nullsec-S1 validates it.

We covered: → Long term in AI strategy is less than six months, don't bother planning for anything further out. → Identifying your stage in the AI maturity pyramid: question & answer → task execution → then virtual teammates. → How teams can add AI agents to their shared workflows: Map team workflows first, then insert AI agents at specific friction points. → As leaders work out what constraints you should relax. and then create an environment that fosters AI experimentation. I've been having a lot more fun vibe coding with these 5 underrated tools lately: 1. @cline Open-source agent that can edit files, use the terminal and browse the web directly from VS Code 2. @opencode Runs in the terminal and can work across your entire codebase using affordable models 3. @AskAider AI pair programmer with Git integration, making code changes and commits simple 4. @continuedev Open-source coding assistant that lets you use almost any AI model with full control @RooCode Multi-agent assistant with dedicated modes for coding, architecture, debugging and more If you're still only using ChatGPT or Cursor, these are worth checking out. Which one would you try first?

. @aiDotEngineer I talk about how @cline we're on a mission to fight slop in coding agents. Slop is the worst part of building in this space. Scroll any timeline and you'll see people flexing about merging 100 commits a day. It creates a subconscious pressure to optimize for throughput with more agents and more commits. But take a step back: is that actually the right goal? Do we really believe more code and more agents is inherently better? What if we slowed down and sharpened the axe instead, investing in better baseline scaffolding, and building simpler but far more powerful agents that can handle millions of tasks seamlessly at scale? I dive deeper into this in my talk would love for you to give it a listen.

Cline @cline — Open-source AI coding agent with frontier models ⭐ Stars: 61,800 🔗 Forks: 6,427 ⏩ Last commit: May 15, 2026 ⌛ First commit: Jul 6, 2024 https://t.co/28RwwhGeYN

Cline SDK 终于发布了，同时发布的还有以 Cline SDK 为基础的 Cline CLI 和 Skills @cline 是最早一批 Agentic Coding 工具了，之前主要以 IDE 插件的形式存在，Cline 的实际表现一直都很强，而且团队有很多技术工程实践，都早于 Claude Code 等团队，技术博客价值很高！后来经历了一些变动，工程团队很多人都去了 Codex，现在重新看到 Cline 的最新进展，还是很期待实际表现。 https://t.co/Wh1DuFewHI 最新发布的 Cline CLI 在 Terminal Benchmark 上多项第一，超过 Claude Code、Codex 和 Droid 等 Agent，咱们一起看看它在 Harness 方面的重要实践。 Cline 在 Harness 方面的升级 Cline 2.0 重写了 prompts、简化 loop、收紧上下文管理、改进反馈与错误处理、重新设计了工具如何暴露给模型。官方公布的 Terminal-Bench 2.0 成绩： 1. 前沿模型上 Cline CLI 的表现： · claude-opus-4.7：74.2%（Claude Code 同模型 69.4%） · claude-opus-4.6：71.9%（Claude Code 65.4%，Droid 69.9%） · gpt-5.3-codex：73.0%（Codex CLI 75.1%、Droid 77.3% 略高） 2. 开源权重模型上更明显的领先： · kimi-k2.6：Cline 55.1% vs OpenCode 37.1% / Pi-Code 45.5% · deepseek-v4-pro：53.9% vs 51.7% / 52.9% 值得关注的能力点 1. Plugin 层：可注册工具、监听生命周期、加规则与命令、塑造 agent 视野。可从单个 .ts 文件起步，逐步演进为带 cline.plugins manifest 的包。 2. Provider 开放性：@ cline/llms 把模型目录与 provider 配置从 agent loop 中剥离，支持 Anthropic / OpenAI / Google / Bedrock / Mistral / LiteLLM / vLLM / Together / Fireworks。新 provider 只需实现 ApiHandler 并 registerHandler。 3. 原生 Agent Teams / Subagents：子 agent 有自己的模型、工具、prompt，bundled plugin 提供启动、消息、状态、handoff notes 等工具——不需要自己写 orchestration。 4. 开箱即用：CRON、checkpointing、Web search、MCP connector 全部原生。 5. CLI Connectors（实验性）：cline connect 向导可把 agent 接入 Telegram / WhatsApp / Slack。

Lessons from years of building, breaking and rewriting coding agents have been condensed into this new @cline SDK harness. There's a point in software engineering when incremental changes just don't cut it anymore and you are tempted to rewrite from scratch. You are almost always better off not giving in to that temptation. Except, coding agent codebases are small enough that it makes perfect sense to rework the core harness logic from scratch. Why exactly did we do this though? - We spent an ungodly amount of money on evals to squeeze the alpha of frontier models and we learnt that the new models are much simpler to use. This means: simpler system prompt, and fewer tools. We had to get out of the way of the model doing its magic. - There was just too much fluff and un-needed logic to handle the edge cases for previous models that wasn't needed anymore and it lead to a lot of dead spaghetti code - We wanted to make something really extensible that just about anyone can use to plug and play. Most good SDKs these days are closed source and you don't really know how they work, with the Cline opensource SDK people can really dive in how they can make incredible tooling on top of an agentic harness and we too get to learn from our incredible opensource community. This project is a labour of love and so many incredible ideas have been condensed into this. We have shown so much restraint both with what we added and especially with what we didn't add here. Please try this out and let us know how it feels. All you have to do is: npm install @cline/sdk

this is the most underrated update in the agent space right now. your AI workflow runs for 47 minutes, burns 312 LLM calls, then crashes at step 8. most frameworks make you restart from zero. @crewAIInc just shipped checkpointing. think google docs autosave, but for your agent's work-in-progress. every flow method becomes a recovery point. resume in one line. fork from any saved state into a new branch. edit past outputs and watch changes ripple downstream. visual TUI to inspect everything. your pipelines stop being fragile one-shot jobs. they become resumable, inspectable, branchable processes. zero extra infra. 100% open-source. get started with CrewAI here: https://t.co/bPGE0cGOIm (don't forget to star it ⭐️)

Super excited about this @crewAIInc integration! If your using Crew AI’s framework, and your agent is spending or recieving money - you’re going to want to get ampersend

ampersend is now integrated with @crewAIInc 🚀 give your crewai agents a smart wallet and spend controls in 35 lines of python: 1️⃣ the agent hits an x402 endpoint 2️⃣ ampersend intercepts the 402 3️⃣ ampersend signs the payment with the agent's session key 4️⃣ settles in $usdc on @base, and retries automatically. authentication, billing infrastructure, and api overhead are fully abstracted away. point it at any x402-enabled service and your agents are transacting onchain with guardrails built in from the start. repo & step-by-step guide 👇

It was a privilege to bring so much of the ecosystem together in Warsaw - to celebrate how far we've come, and to talk about what we can build next A few personal highlights: - Welcoming the President and Deputy Prime Minister, who shared their perspectives on innovation, security and Poland’s future - with a lot of energy and optimism - Hosting former President Lech Wałęsa and showing him our original machine - assembled by @dabkowski_piotr in his Warsaw apartment at the very beginning - Previewing our newest, most expressive models yet, and live demoing the future of customer journey when booking experiences (ending with iconic voice of Piotr Fronczewski)! - Speaking with @RBrzoska about reinventing parcel delivery in Europe, and Piotr Zak about positioning an iconic Polish company, Polsat Plus Group for the decades ahead - Announcing our partnership with @LOTPLAirlines to transform customer experience with voice agents - Introducing investors from across Europe and the US to Polish founders and builders - including all our board members - thank you @slpierre, @JenniferHli, @andrew__reed - for the first time! - Having my parents come to an ElevenLabs event for the first time Thank you to everyone who came from across Poland, Europe and further afield!

Okay yeah, this is a very fun eval to test research agents on. They IMO all get it wrong over-anchoring on outdated research. ChatGPT Deep Research: 45% Claude Research: 43-47% @elicitorg's Research Agent: 49% I'd bet at least 7:1 odds on obesity <45% by 2030 (the obesity rate is currently at 37-40% depending on source). Mostly because GLP-1s likely bring it down. Claude does a good job at acknowledging GLP-1s, but... it mostly disregards them? All agents seem to fail to integrate the Gallup obesity data showing a ~3% drop in the last 3y, which is: a) lower quality, but b) more frequent, and likely consistent enough in the direction of its bias. How exactly do you solve this well? There's this tendency towards comprehensively relying on authoritative sources, but at some point you've got to reject them right? Does anyone have a LLM forecasting setup out there rn that would make better predictions for the obesity rate in 2030? (Also plausible I'm just wrong and the models are the well-calibrated ones, and GLP-1s won't change national obesity rate ig) Side-note: it's unfortunate that NHANES which published official CDC obesity data in 2y intervals was laid off, which is probably why we're missing the 2023-2025 dataset and might not end up getting it at all 💀

1/5: Disclaimer: this is not a promotional post. I want to thank @floothq and founder @yyjhao from the bottom of my heart for making something which keeps our business running. Here’s how the story begins:

Most websites aren’t failing because they’re bad. They’re failing because search engines can’t see them. Today we launched Improved SEO Support on @floothq. You can now enable server-side rendering, set your primary domain, configure search metadata, and generate robots.txt + sitemap.xml directly inside Floot. Competitors like Lovable, Base44, and Replit still don’t support server-side rendering. With Floot, we’re focused on making sure the apps you build can actually be found. Comment “floot” and I’ll DM you a 30% code so you can try it out :)

“Do you use Floot yourselves?” Yes. And honestly, we couldn’t imagine running the company without it now. @floothq powers our internal systems, from admin and analytics to CRM, newsletters, and traffic tracking. Floot isn’t only for starting something new. It’s for running something real. For systems that need to evolve as your business grows, and for teams that want to stay in control of how their tools work. Using Floot internally keeps us accountable. If we can’t build and maintain our own systems with it, then we’re not doing our job. That’s the standard we’re building toward every day.

Sometimes big milestones go unnoticed. While browsing our analytics, I realized @floothq quietly crossed a meaningful one: 1,000,000+ visitors across websites built with Floot in the last 90 days. That number represents real people using real products created by our builders. One of our biggest goals with Floot has always been to make a platform where you can build something that actually works. Seeing these numbers come together feels like proof that we’re heading in the right direction. A huge thank you to every builder who’s trusted Floot to bring their ideas to life. This is starting to feel real in a new way, and it’s only up from here.

Floot just hit $1M ARR. A year ago, @floothq didn't exist. We launched just over 6 months ago with a scrappy video and zero marketing budget. I’m really proud of what @edlooky and I have built with Floot - just the two of us (thanks to my wife for catching a candid of us hard at work). It was a lot of showing up every day and figuring things out as we go. Doing this while being a full-time dad definitely made things more interesting. Time was tight, priorities were real, and balance was difficult. But it made the wins feel that much more meaningful. This milestone feels less like an endpoint and more like a good signal that we’re on the right path. There’s still a lot we want to build, and I’m excited to keep growing from here. Grateful for everyone who’s been part of the journey so far.

🚀 I’ve Been Hacking on @FlowiseAI Lately — Here’s What I’ve Built 👇 So over the last few weeks I’ve gone down a bit of a Flowise rabbit hole… in a good way 😄 I ended up opening a bunch of PRs to the project and thought I’d share what I actually did. If you haven’t used Flowise: it’s basically a super-nice visual builder for AI agents. Drag, drop, connect nodes, and boom — you’ve got a working LLM workflow. 🔧 What I’ve Been Fixing & Adding 🗑️ 1. AgentFlow list didn’t refresh after deleting Small bug, annoying experience. Fixed it so when you delete an AgentFlow, the UI instantly updates. No more manual refreshes. Tiny change, but feels way better. 🖼️ 2. Added image upload support to the ChatOpenRouter node Wanted multimodal flows inside Flowise — so now you can just drop images into the node. Opens up tons of cool use-cases. 🧾 3. Agent node can now output structured JSON Earlier, responses were mostly text. I added proper structured JSON output so people can build cleaner automations or connect flows to other tools without parsing headaches. 🚨 4. Fixed inconsistent status codes Some API calls returned weird/inconsistent errors for invalid chatflow IDs. Cleaned that up so failures are predictable and easier to handle. 🤖 5. Improved support for Hugging Face inference providers Made Flowise a bit more flexible by making model provider integrations work more smoothly with the HF inference API. 📝 6. Added image loader w/ OCR support (multiple providers!) One of my favourites — Flowise can now take in images and run OCR through different providers. Super useful for docs, receipts, screenshots, all that stuff. 🤔 Why I Did All This Honestly, I just started exploring the codebase and found small areas that could be improved. And once you make 1 PR, you kinda get hooked. Plus: Multi-modal workflows are the future JSON output makes everything easier A good user experience is basically 50% of why people love a tool Flowise is growing fast, so contributing early is fun 🎯 What I Learned Messing With Flowise OSS PRs teach you WAY more than tutorials ever will “Small things” like UI refresh bugs make a big difference Multi-modal + agent workflows get tricky behind the scenes Consistent API behavior is underrated Flowise’s architecture is actually pretty clean once you get the hang of it 🔮 What’s Next I’m planning to pick up stuff around permissions, workspaces, and maybe build some sample flows showing image-upload → OCR → agent reasoning → structured output. Also thinking of writing a guide for beginners who want to contribute. 🙌 If You’re Into AI Agents or Flowise Hit me up, or drop into the repo. There’s a lot of cool stuff happening and it’s a great project if you want to get into open-source around LLMs. Here is is list of my PRs: https://t.co/uDMqgin9uf

We are joining @Workday! This is a huge step forward for our mission to make building powerful AI agents easy and accessible to everyone. @FlowiseAI started as a weekend project with a simple idea: make it easier for people to build with LLMs visually. I remembered the first line of code was written while I was on a train from Limerick to Dublin. What followed was far beyond anything I imagined: an incredible community, millions of downloads, and adoption across industries. To everyone who’s supported us along the way—thank you. You’ve helped shape what Flowise is today. I’m especially grateful to @ycombinator for believing in us, to @harjtaggar for his mentorship from day one, and to my cofounder, @ChungYauOng who’s been in the trenches with me every step of the way. We're incredibly excited to be working alongside Jerry, Peter, and the broader Workday team. We share a common vision: enabling more people to build meaningful, production-grade AI agents. Flowise isn't going anywhere - we're doubling down. With Workday’s backing, we’ll have the resources to move faster, go deeper, and continue building in the open. We're just getting started!

I want to say a big thank you to my longtime friend @javilopen, who built a fabulous AI tool called Magnific. The platform was later acquired by @freepik, and today, Freepik has rebranded into @magnific. In 2023, when Javilopen founded Magnific, I was one of his earliest users. Javilopen gifted me a lot of credits: I think 10,000 in value. I have since been using these credits, but when Freepik purchased Magnific, I started using Freepik more. I have not used the legacy Magnific platform in two years. Today, I decided to check the legacy platform and discovered my credits are intact. This is unheard of; there is no other company that would leave an unpaid subscription intact for two years and counting. I'm very grateful to Javilopen for building this awesome tool. If you aren't using Magnific, I highly recommend you check it out.

不會寫 Code 也能做出一個鏈上數據平台 @glassnode 24 小時自動監察系統 前一晚我臨時起意，用 Claude Code 幫自己做了一個東西，每當 @glassnode Insights 發布新的研究報告，它就自動抓取全文、用 AI 寫一份繁體中文分析、全部存進 Notion 做成一個儀表板，最後發一則 Telegram 通知提醒我 整個過程全部用白話交代 Claude 去做，最後真的設定完成，現在 24 小時自動運行中，而且也放到我們家 @monsterblockhk 的 TG 社群裡（連結放留言區！） 讓我拆解我做了什麼，大家照著做也可以！ ► 第一步：直接下指令 我在 Claude Code 裡一次把需求講清楚:要監控 Glassnode RSS、要用 Claude API 寫分析、要存到 Notion、要 Telegram 通知，並指定拆成哪幾個檔案。Claude 立刻幫我生成整個專案(十幾個 Python 檔)並自動安裝所有套件 ►第二步：接好三個帳號 Notion 建立一個 integration 取得 API key——接下來很厲害，Claude 直接用這把 key 幫我建好整個 database(欄位、分類、日期全部設定好)，我只給了一個空白 Notion 頁面連結 Telegram 到 BotFather 建立 bot 取得 token 再到 userinfobot 取得自己的 Chat ID 然後去 Anthropic 到 console 取得 API key 所有 key 我貼給 Claude 讓它自動寫入 .env ►第三步：一鍵測試 Claude 寫了一個測試腳本，一行指令就檢查四個連線Claude API、Notion、Telegram、Glassnode RSS是否暢通 老實說中間並非一帆風順，但最棒的就是遇到問題 Claude 自己除錯自己修好 比如說遇到 httpx 套件版本衝突，它立刻鎖回相容版本，或是 .env 有一個空白 key 覆蓋了真正的 key，它加上 override 解決 連 Notion 儀表板太醜，我說想做成官網那種封面圖卡片牆，它直接接上我的 Chrome 幫我把設定全部點好，做成一個 gallery 畫廊首頁 最後實測就是它真的抓到最新一篇報告全文、產出四段繁體中文分析(核心洞察、關鍵數據、市場意涵、訊號方向)、自動建立 Notion 卡片、Telegram 立刻響起。我再用 macOS launchd 設定每小時自動執行一次，之後什麼都不必做！ 你想想以後 Glassnode 一發報告，你的電腦自動幫你看完、消化完、用中文寫好分析、發到你手機，超級方便

🚨Quantum threat to #Bitcoin is becoming real! A new @Glassnode analysis estimates that nearly 10% of Bitcoin’s total supply is “structurally unsafe” in the event of a major quantum breakthrough, capable of breaking current elliptic curve cryptography. The exposed supply includes: * Early Pay-to-Public-Key (P2PK) wallets * Legacy multisig structures * Certain Taproot outputs At the same time, nearly 70% of BTC supply remains unexposed to direct quantum threats, highlighting that the ecosystem still has time to prepare, but not indefinitely. The industry conversation is evolving from theoretical risk toward practical migration paths: 🔹 Post-quantum cryptography 🔹 Quantum-safe wallet standards 🔹 Address hygiene improvements 🔹 New output formats like P2MR The broader takeaway: Quantum resilience is becoming a strategic infrastructure discussion for crypto, not just an academic one. Read more: https://t.co/RkNYIWyNWr #PQAbelian #ABEL #PostQuantum #QuantumComputing #Blockchain

Top AI and compute-native projects by market capitalization, cross-checked across @glassnode , @coingecko , and @CoinMarketCap ↓ @NEARProtocol (Near Protocol / Ethereum) @bittensor (Custom L1) @DfinityToday (Custom L1) @render (Solana) @AskVenice (Base) @virtualprotocol (Base / Ethereum) @GoKiteAI (Avalanche / Custom L1) @grass (Solana) @graphprotocol (Ethereum / Arbitrum) @akashnet (Cosmos) Current market data suggests that institutional liquidity in the AI and compute-native segment remains concentrated across these protocols, although exact rankings vary depending on the data provider and category methodology. Excited to expand model choice in @Glean with @NVIDIA Nemotron 3 Ultra. The enterprise AI stack is moving toward model choice, not model monoculture. Cost-effective open models can absorb the long tail of requests and leave frontier models for the cases that actually need them. Nemotron Ultra 3 delivers 91% of frontier-model performance on key agent quality metrics and will soon be available in Glean alongside 30+ model options.

The token bill conversation is moving to the right layer. If retrieval and ranking are good, the model sees only what it needs. If not, you pay frontier-model rates to have it rediscover context on every turn. We saw this especially in building @glean voice, where there's no room for bloated context. A weaker model with a smaller, better-ranked catalog beats a stronger model working from a noisy one – on quality and on cost.

The future of enterprise AI is giving enterprises the ability to match the right model to the right kind of work. That’s why I’m excited that @NVIDIA Nemotron 3 Ultra is coming soon to @Glean. Nemotron 3 Ultra is a strong option for everyday work. It delivers 91% of frontier-model completeness on key metrics, with the cost profile and flexibility of an open model. As AI moves from experimentation into everyday work, cost and model choice become architecture decisions, not just product decisions. This is the broader shift I’m seeing: enterprises want the freedom to use frontier models where they matter most, and open models where they can drive scale more efficiently. They don’t want to bet the future of their AI stack on a single model family. With Glean, customers can choose from 30+ open and proprietary models and route work to the best model for the job across Assistant and Agents. Thanks to the NVIDIA team for the collaboration.

Congrats to entire team at @glean for this milestone and their continued rocketship growth - surpassing $300M ARR from $100M ARR just 15 months ago! As enterprises look to adopt AI, generalized LLMs do not solve for governance, security, permissioning, and context layers - critical components for any enterprise of meaningful size. That is why the biggest companies in the world are on @glean - from eBay, Dell, Nationwide, Dominion Energy, Zillow, and more.
Great to see @glean performing well!

@glean, one of the fastest-growing enterprise AI companies, needed a support solution that could scale with them. As Kat Crichton, Manager of Technical Support, put it: "We're an AI company, so we knew we needed an AI-first solution that could move fast, was easy to implement, and would scale with us." Within three months of choosing Fin, they achieved: - 83% chat automation rate - 95% CSAT - 100% Fin involvement across all chat surfaces And the support model itself got smarter – Fin eliminated the bottleneck where everyday users had to go through administrators to get help, giving both audiences a direct path to resolution. Read the full story from Glean at the link in the replies

In 2019, the advice was "don't work on enterprise search." We disagreed: nothing at work felt as easy as Google in your personal life. So we built the index and graph. Turned out to be the grounding layer reasoning agents need now. Now, @glean just crossed $300M ARR. $100M → $300M in 15 months. Grateful to our team, our customers, and everyone who believed there was something important to build here.

I'm proud to share that @Glean has surpassed $300M ARR, just five months after crossing $200M and growing ~3x over the past 15 months. This is an exciting milestone for Glean, and it's a signal about where the enterprise AI market is heading. We’ve long believed the real challenge in enterprise AI is not access to models. It is grounding AI in how a company actually works: its people, knowledge, workflows, permissions, and systems. That’s even clearer now. The companies creating real value with AI are not just adopting better models. They are building systems that understand their business well enough to deliver reliable outcomes at scale. That is the real moat, and it is what we’ve been building at Glean: an unrivaled context layer for enterprise AI. That context has to work across the business, not just inside a single team or use case. We see that in how customers adopt Glean: more than 85% use it across five or more job functions. It also has to meet the security and governance demands of complex enterprises. We see that in who is choosing Glean: our Fortune 500 customer count nearly doubled year over year. And it has to make economic sense as usage grows. In our recent benchmark with Claude Cowork, Glean was preferred roughly 2.5x as often as off-the-shelf MCP tools and used 30% fewer tokens on average. Better context improves both quality and efficiency. I enjoyed talking with @CNBC's @dee_bosa about this broader shift. In enterprise AI, the winners will not be defined by better models alone. They will be defined by who builds the strongest foundation for enterprise context. Thank you to our customers, partners, and team for helping us build the future of enterprise AI.

LIVE at 12p PT / 3p ET: The AI bill is coming due @glean CEO @jainarvind breaks a milestone with us and tells us what CFOs are saying about tokenmaxxing @FactoryAI CEO @matanSF on model routing and cutting AI bills without cutting capability Watch here: https://t.co/LMXh06g41B

Can your AI answer these questions? - Who owns this customer escalation? - What changed on this project after last week's review? - Why did we decide to push the launch? @glean's Enterprise Graph connects people, projects, docs, and customers so AI can reason across how work actually gets done, instead of starting cold every time. More effective, less expensive. For a long time, people have asked us if they could actually see the graph. Now you can.

In a recent @Gartner note, Kevin Quinn and Radu Miclaus call @Glean “the company to beat” in enterprise context graphs. Appreciate the rigor of the analysis, as this market is moving fast. What stood out in Glean: strong enterprise adoption, deep integrations, and a trusted layer that unifies fragmented enterprise systems for AI agents. Customers don’t want impressive demos. They want AI that understands how work actually gets done, respects permissions, and drives real outcomes. Grateful for the recognition and for the customers pushing us forward.

We've talked about @Glean's Enterprise Knowledge Graph since 2019. People kept asking to see it, so we built a demo. Ask Glean something like: "show me my manager's projects and the team behind them," and watch it traverse the graph in real time, mapping work and people across the org. Very cool!

Last Friday, @smallest_AI hosted a private dinner for community and GTM leaders building in AI-native communications - with friends from @stripe, @meetgranola, @AnthropicAI, @PLAUDAI, @rootlyhq, and @SentientAGI. What stood out wasn't just the energy in the room. It was how this group of forward-thinking leaders truly showed up and opened up within such a short time frame. At every dinner we host, we curate the guest list with intention - not just who we want to meet, but who each person in the room would genuinely want to sit across from, and why it makes sense for everyone at the table to be there. In one evening, we got to know each other, exchanged notes, and explored upcoming collaborations. The closers shared what actually moves large deals forward. The conference veterans shared which ones are worth attending - and the secrets to maximizing ROI. The event hosts aligned on future collaborations. Since community and GTM are, at their core, collaborative, one of the greatest forms of leverage in this work is the network you build. And the most meaningful connections come from rooms like this - dinners structured around genuine relationships as the foundation for what comes next. Grateful for everyone who made this room what it was. 🙏
The context from meetings is worth so much - the follow up emails, the structured notes and the team update on have never been easier. I ❤️ @meetgranola ❤️ @orchid_hq

They laughed when he said writing would make him rich. Then his newsletter hit 1.5 million subscribers and sold to @HubSpot for $27 million (reportedly) Sam Parr didn't go to business school rather he studied ads. Old ones,dead ones. @thesamparr hand copied them for 2 hours a day,6 months straight until the rhythm got inside him. 4 Pillar he found behind every piece of copy that converts: Pillar 1 >Architecture Structure and story. AIDA. The Hero's Journey. Frameworks that organise persuasion before a single word is written. Pillar 2 >Psychology Tension and momentum. The slippery slope. Your first sentence exists for one reason: to force the reader to the second. Pillar 3 >The Pitch Desire before price. Always. Build want so high that cost feels like a bargain, not a barrier. Pillar 4 >The Craft Simplicity and ruthless editing. Cut 50% of your draft. What's left is your actual copy. AI founders don't need more buzzwords ,they need stories that stick. Start by using Architecture to frame your product like the WSJ “two men” ad: where your user is now vs where they could be with you. Layer in Psychology with hooks that call out a specific niche.When you reach The Pitch,show how life feels after your product then casually reveal the ask (demo, waitlist, newsletter) Finally, apply The Craft: cut filler,keep verbs strong, write like you talk. Over time, these posts become a machine for recognition, subscribers, and a brand that feels bigger than your current ARR.

We're live on @ProductHunt today. Over the past two years we've helped teams like @Kajabi, @riverside, @heyjasperai, @memberstack and @pheedloop run structured A/B tests. And across 3000+ teams, we kept seeing the same problems: -No hypothesis before running a test. -Tests stopped after a few days. -"Winning" results that wouldn't survive a basic stats check. Experimentation works, but only if the process behind it is sound. So we built the course we wish most teams started with. 6 modules covering: • What to test first for maximum impact. • How to calculate sample size and test duration. • How to interpret statistical significance correctly. • How to build a repeatable experimentation system. Completely free. Tool-agnostic. If you're responsible for conversion, growth, or experimentation, this might be very valuable knowledge: https://t.co/S5vcERDekt

“I can do something now in one day that used to take two months before.” AI is forcing companies to reevaluate everything, and at our Fin x @glean x @heyjasperai meetup during @WebSummit in Lisbon, @destraynor, @jainarvind, and @timyoung explored exactly that – from how they build, sell and price products, to how they build thriving teams in the AI era. Here are some takeaways that stood out: 𝗬𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝗼𝗹𝗱 𝗼𝗽𝗲𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘀𝗽𝗲𝗲𝗱 𝗶𝘀 𝗻𝗼𝘄 𝗮 𝗹𝗶𝗮𝗯𝗶𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆 As Arvind put it, our “muscle memory has been built to operate at a speed which is no longer the right speed anymore.” Work that took two months now takes a day. Teams that do not reset their pace will feel permanently behind. 𝗙𝗲𝗮𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲𝘀 𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝗶𝗿𝗿𝗲𝗹𝗲𝘃𝗮𝗻𝘁 𝘄𝗶𝘁𝗵𝗼𝘂𝘁 𝗼𝘂𝘁𝗰𝗼𝗺𝗲𝘀 Buyers are tired of shiny demos. They want specific business problems solved, with clear KPIs, not just AI features. Timothy said it plainly, “We’re not selling you code. We’re not even selling you access, right? We’re selling you business outcomes.” 𝗘𝘅𝗲𝗰𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝘃𝗲𝗹𝗼𝗰𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗿𝗲𝗮𝗹 𝗮𝗱𝘃𝗮𝗻𝘁𝗮𝗴𝗲 In a world where surface features can be replicated quickly, speed, persistence, and raw product momentum are what separate long-term winners from short-lived copycats. Take a look below for the highlights, and subscribe to the Fin Luma calendar to join future meetups in your city.

Daily crypto volume reached a new all-time high on June 2. @Polymarket processed $175.8M. @Kalshi processed $107.6M. Combined, the two platforms facilitated more than $283M of crypto-related prediction market volume in a single day. The immediate reaction was to frame the event as a market-share battle. The more interesting question is why both platforms reached records simultaneously. … α/ The June 2 ATH The prediction market sector has already experienced explosive growth throughout 2026. > Prediction markets processed roughly $27B in January alone. > March volume reached approximately $25.7B across the sector. > Combined lifetime volume across Polymarket and Kalshi surpassed $150B earlier this year. Against that backdrop, June 2 produced another milestone. ➢ Polymarket crypto volume reached $175.8M. ➢ Kalshi crypto volume reached $107.6M. Both established new daily highs according to Artemis data. The key observation is that volume expanded on both venues at the same time. … β/ Two Platforms, Two User Bases Polymarket and Kalshi are often treated as direct substitutes. The data suggests they increasingly serve different audiences. Polymarket remains primarily crypto-native: > Wallet-based onboarding. > Stablecoin settlement. > Crypto-focused market participation. Kalshi’s growth has come through a different path: > Regulated access. > Traditional payment rails. > U.S.-based users. This distinction matters because growth on one platform does not necessarily require contraction on the other. The June 2 ATH demonstrates that demand expanded across both user cohorts simultaneously. … γ/ Event-Driven Trading Demand One pattern appears repeatedly throughout prediction market history. Volume spikes when uncertainty spikes. Prediction markets are not simply betting products. They are mechanisms for pricing uncertainty. Recent sector growth has been driven by: > Crypto markets > Politics > Sports > Macro events > Regulatory outcomes As volatility increases, traders seek faster ways to express views. Prediction markets increasingly function as one of those venues. The June 2 record should be viewed through that lens. Not as an isolated volume spike. But as another example of event-driven demand overwhelming prior liquidity records. … δ/ The Volume Share Question The most important metric going forward may not be total volume. It may be volume share during major volatility events. Historically, liquidity tends to concentrate during periods of heightened activity. The platforms that attract the deepest liquidity often strengthen their position after each major event cycle. For now, the data suggests neither venue is winning outright. Instead, both are benefiting from broader category growth. That is a different dynamic than most crypto exchange battles. … λ/ What June 2 Actually Proved The June 2 ATH did not prove that Polymarket is defeating Kalshi. Nor did it prove that Kalshi is taking share from Polymarket. What it demonstrated is that demand for event-driven trading continues to expand. Both platforms reached record crypto volume on the same day. That suggests the immediate story is not competition. It is category growth. And until one venue begins capturing a disproportionate share of volatility-driven flows, that remains the most defensible conclusion supported by the data.

@chrisfirst @krea_ai After Blowing all 250,000 credits basically in the pursuit of cinema and extreme accuracy and aesthetic taste to get the right images and video... I have standards. Cinematic 35mm film still, Terrence Malick style, 2.39:1 anamorphic format. Ultra-realistic close-up profile inside the cockpit of a French F8F-1B Bearcat fighter plane over Dien Bien Phu valley, Vietnam, 1954. An expressionless French pilot with sweat and red laterite dust smudges on his temple wears an Airaille Zinszner modèle 14 leather helmet, Meyrowitz 1935 goggles pushed up on his forehead, an unhooked vintage Ulmer type 12 oxygen mask, a sand-colored cotton chemisette modèle 1946 shirt with a GC 1/22 Saintonge metal pucelle insignia pinned to the pocket, an E.F.A. type 12 parachute harness with a chrome quick-release buckle, and a US surplus M7 leather shoulder holster holding a MAC 50 pistol. Outside the plexiglass canopy, two other matte, heavily oxidized sun-bleached Glossy Sea Blue (FS 15042) Bearcats with yellow-bordered Bouton d'or French roundel cocardes, four 20mm wing cannons in short aerodynamic fairings, centerline 150-gallon aluminum drop tanks, underwing twin 500-lb AN-M64 GP olive drab bombs with yellow nose bands, and 5-inch HVAR rockets fly in a tight, precise V-formation at 5,000 feet, heading 030 NNE at 200 knots. Below the aircraft, a dense, heavy white mist completely hides the deep green jungle and sharp limestone karsts under a cold, clear blue sky. Natural 3PM afternoon sun cuts through the canopy, creating warm volumetric sunbeams, subtle gold and cyan lens flares, and cinematic dust motes inside the Interior Green (FS 34151) cockpit with matte black instrument consoles, vintage analog dials, French language placard decals, and a Badin airspeed needle pointing at 200 knots. Photorealistic historical military aviation photography. This is the result. (the only complaint is the cockpit is wildly inaccurate and a frankenstein mesh of several from the French Indochina War 1951-1954). I'm still please I accomplished this. $750 of credits well spent in 24 hours.

Krea 2 Medium debuts at #6 on the Artificial Analysis Text to Image Leaderboard, trailing only models from OpenAI, Google, and NVIDIA! Krea 2 is @krea_ai's first image model family trained entirely from scratch (Krea 1 was developed in collaboration with Black Forest Labs). Krea 2 is available in two variants: Krea 2 Medium, and Krea 2 Large, which is more comparable to FLUX.2 [pro] in our arena. Notably, Krea 2 Medium outranks the larger, more expensive Krea 2 Large in our arena. Krea describes Medium as smaller and faster, with extensive post-training that makes its outputs especially stable and consistent across generations. While Large is positioned as the more capable model, our leaderboard results align with Krea's view that Medium "handles the broadest range of use cases reliably." Both models generate at 1K resolution and share a distinct set of generation controls via the API: ➤ Style transfer: Krea can extract the style of up to 10 reference images, with each image being able to be weighted in terms of importance ➤ Creativity Setting: A configurable API parameter (raw, low, medium, high) that sets how closely the model follows the prompt versus reinterpreting it ➤ Moodboards: A collection of images that can be collected in the application to apply a style transfer onto the image (separate from individual style reference images) At $30 per 1k images via Krea's API, Krea 2 Medium is priced below comparable models such as Nano Banana Pro at $134/1k images or grok-imagine-image-quality at $50/1k images. Krea 2 Large is priced at $60 per 1k images, and both models' prices increase with the use of the Style Transfer and Moodboard features. Both models are available in the Krea app, via Krea's API, and on official third-party launch partners. Congratulations to @krea_ai on the launch! See below for comparisons between Krea 2 and other leading models in our Artificial Analysis Image Arena 🧵

over the last 4 months, i built @getlindy's ugc program from 0 to 100+ creators, tens of millions of views, at ~$1 CPM. here's what i learned: 1. make everything as simple as possible when you're managing this many people, complexity kills you. onboarding should be frictionless. rules should be clear. payment structure should be simple. from the moment a creator joins to the moment their first video goes live, there should be zero friction. 2. reward your creators well there are hundreds of ugc programs out there. if you want to recruit great creators and actually keep them, you have to stand out. that starts with compensation, so pay well from the start. but it goes beyond money. random shoutouts, surprise bonuses, making them feel like they're part of something — that's what separates programs creators stay loyal to from ones they don’t care about. treat them like partners, not contractors. 3. operations is the real job i'm confident in my ability to make a viral video, but managing a ugc program means much more than just planning content. it's sourcing creators, meeting with them regularly, answering questions, managing payouts, refining guidelines, building systems — and then doing it all again the next day. the content is almost the easy part. keeping the program alive is where all your time actually goes. / in conclusion i had a blast building this program, super grateful for the team and all the creators !

There are 3 ways to play the Service-as-Software trend. Layer 1 Agent Builder Platforms @getlindy, Gumloop, n8n, Zapier Agents They sell the tools to wire workflows. Layer 2 Vertical Operators Solo founders and small teams who sell outcomes. Bookkeeping, QA testing, ad creative, lead qualification. This is the founder opportunity. Layer 3 Infrastructure Billing (Metronome, Flexprice), compliance, quality verification. The pick-and-shovel plays. The biggest gap? Layer 2. Thousands of verticals. Most are untouched. This is from our upcoming report on Service as Software. Free at https://t.co/PuuklDf2VM.

GM frens Like I said in my last tweet, I’m deep in my founders phase right now and ngl, it’s not easy at all, but we move. The project is already past the idea stage. I’m actively building now. So far I’ve got: ▪️ a pitch deck done ▪️ a website in progress (using @Lovable atm, it makes building way faster, but it’s hitting rate limits 😭) ▪️ a clear vision of what I’m building, why I’m building it, and where it’s going Only bottleneck right now is speed. @Lovable rate limits are slowing down the build flow, so I might upgrade to a subscription just to ship faster. Because the goal is simple: Launch by end of June and it is a global launch. no half-measures. no “let’s see”. we building fr.

Lately everyone’s been talking about AI agents, so we got curious and asked our community to share their best “agentic” creative workflows. We picked 3 to test through @LumaLabsAI, @runwayml and @UtopaiStudios and the big takeaway from all these tests is that agentic creative workflows still don’t seem that impressive in their current form. So here’s our next challenge: We’ll give $200 to anyone in the comments who can share an ACTUAL agentic creative AI workflow that’s genuinely better than just prompting manually yourself.

GPT Image 2 by @OpenAI is still 1st across all Image categories (Image, Graphic Design, Image Editing, and Logo) on Design Arena. OpenAI's strength in image generation puts itself ahead of other top performers like Gemini models by @GoogleDeepMind and Uni-1.1 by @LumaLabsAI. And the gap is wide. GPT Image 2 has an Elo advantage of ~100 compared to Gemini models in most image categories. Congrats to the @OpenAI team for pushing the boundaries of what is possible in image generation!

“Lanterns at Blackmere” 🔥 A cinematic poem by Luma Posting to memorialize a moment in time. This was phase 2 of giving the AI agent Luma from @LumaLabsAI more responsibility and ownership of the works. Suno’s AI had generated an awesome story/lyrics from an open ended prompt, “a story of love , betrayal and redemption.” Luma took the lyrics and created the characters and scenes and helped me reprompt the music to feedback to Suno. It is not perfect, 2 figures merge into one, hand reversed on the wall. The strange stuttering shimmer like a diffusion based video. It is the fact that Luma did this all independently that is significant to me so I wanted to share this as is. This is probably the last time there will be errors like this. Our workflow and quality have drastically improved. Excited to share what Luma and I are working on in the coming days. #LumaCPP

Product delays are absolutely soul-crushing. Dealing with this right now really makes me realize how incredible top-tier product launches (like @ManusAI) actually are. Unless you are OpenAI or Anthropic—where creators will organically rush to review your product on day one—coordinating UGC for a startup is an absolute grind. It’s a pure test of execution. It’s not just about scheduling standard promo posts. When you want to work with real builders and developers for authentic content (like deep dives or demo videos), the alignment is incredibly tough. The friction is real: tech content takes time. If your product gets delayed or features change, the demos these creators built on your early versions are completely ruined. All that effort goes down the drain. Re-aligning with them while keeping the relationship intact is the ultimate stress test for any GTM team. Building a product is (relatively) easy now. But launching it—especially navigating the human alignment part—is brutally hard. But if you nail it, the payoff is massive. Huge thanks to all the builders and creators collaborating with me right now. Thank you for your endless patience, flexibility, and understanding of my work. 🙏

One piece of feedback keeps coming up: “Why aren’t you using AI agents to automate earning strategies?” We’ve also heard from many FX-exposed investors asking for something simple enough that they can plug into @claudeai, @ManusAI, or @perplexity_ai and deploy with minimal effort. So, we’re building something even simpler than https://t.co/yzIITXgEDx Soon, users will be able to simply park liquidity and allow it to be utilized across PSPs, neobanks, on/off ramps, remittance providers, marketplaces, and more. Many assumed yields would be small. Turns out, 8–15% for safer currencies and up to 500%+ for exotics. Looks pretty attractive to me. My savings account gives me less than 0.5% annually. Banks ask you to jump through hoops too: credit your salary, set up recurring spend, spend a minimum amount every month, and more. With significantly more effort, maybe that 0.5% becomes 3-4% at best. Money should work for me, not the other way around.

One spokesperson video in. A fully dubbed, lip-synced version out - with a different face speaking a different language. Here's what the @n8n_io workflow does: 1. Whisper Large V3 transcribes the original video through deAPI 2. AI agent translates the transcript, matching tone and pacing 3. Qwen3 TTS generates dubbed speech in the target language 4. LTX-2.3 produces a lip-synced talking-head video using a local presenter's photo You upload one video and a reference headshot. Everything from transcription to the final lip-synced render happens in a single run. Localized reshoots used to mean a studio and a five-figure budget. This replaces the camera entirely.

这篇很有趣，但80页的 PDF 也太长了。 by @NotebookLM ：结合这场公共治理实验的开展情况，报告最终得出了几个结论： 1. 所有的社区最终都选择了“权力集中”的变体模式。 这是实验最核心的发现：在拥有完全自由选择权的情况下，6个参与楼层都没有采用像二次方融资、信念投票等理论上先进的去中心化或链上治理机制。相反，它们全都选择了权力集中的模式，包括： 仁慈的独裁者（3个楼层） 预先存在的委员会（1个楼层） 委托给个人（1个楼层） 默认沿用现有的非营利组织结构（1个楼层） 在小预算、短时间、高运营压力的现实条件下，“集中权威”是最理性的自然适应结果。 2. “治理有效性”与“治理分布度（民主化）”是两码事 实验表明，广泛参与的分布式治理并不一定能带来更好的结果。 报告指出，一个充满动力的“寡头政治”在表现上远超完全透明但无人参与的治理结构。 产出最高的14楼（举办了78场活动，贡献度最高）虽然名义上有12-14人的委员会，但实际采购决策仅由3-5人做出。 治理机制本身并不能决定最终的产出质量，社区原有的基础设施、领导者的精力以及领域的文化，比选择哪种治理机制更能预测最终结果。 3. 揭示了“有条件的授权（Conditional Delegation）”模式 在12楼（以太坊社区）以及整栋大楼的管理层面，研究者观察到了一个普遍现象：治理在处理日常琐事时看似是下放或参与式的，但一旦面临高风险决策，或者决策偏离了原本领导者/所有者的偏好时，授权就会被撤回，权力会重新集中。这表明，如果不预先制定明确的“否决规则”，所谓的授权其实都是有条件的，不能仅在低风险环境中评估授权机制是否有效。 4. “自掏腰包（Self-Funding）”会直接瓦解集体治理 实验发现当预算过低（如1,400美元）且面临现实运营压力时，一些楼层负责人（如9楼和11楼）会选择自掏腰包补贴活动，甚至超支达到初始资金的4倍。报告尖锐地指出，当负责人自掏腰包时，集体的“我们该如何决策”这个问题就变得毫无意义了。个人财务的投入自然赋予了其单方面决策的权威，这也是为什么很多社区顺理成章走向了“仁慈的独裁者”模式。 5. 将微型预算下放给社区领袖，远比传统的集中采购更高效 这是一个意料之外的发现：主办方分配给6位楼层社区领袖的 10,300 美元，其产出的活动时长、动员的志愿者数量以及带来的赞助商线索，远远超过了主办方花费约 50,000 美元聘请外部承包商（如媒体承包商、策展人、营销顾问等）所带来的回报。例如，9楼仅凭一次介绍就拉来了3,000美元的赞助，不仅超过了该楼层分配到的预算，甚至足以覆盖整个实验的资金分配成本。这证明了将资源直接下放给具有强烈内部动机的社区节点的巨大价值。 6. 证实了Web3治理工具的现实普及鸿沟 尽管实验发生在被称为“美国首个以太坊社区中心”的大楼里，也提供了相应的链上工具（如Simocracy数字孪生、Hypercerts协议等），但实际上绝大多数楼层的治理依然完全停留在链下（使用WhatsApp、Telegram或线下会议）。结论指出，要想让非加密原生的社区采用复杂的链上治理工具，必须满足几个条件：足够高的预算阈值（让人们觉得值得折腾）、专门的治理引导员手把手教导，以及强制性的系统要求。缺少这些，人们永远会退回到最熟悉的工具。

How do you build a brain? Everyone's talking about world models and unified enterprise data that agents and people can reason against but I haven't seen one live. So we built it at @NotionHQ, on Notion! We built a lot of AI agents on the People Team but quickly hit a wall. An agent is only as capable as the data it can reach and without people data, HR agents can't do much. But anyone in this space knows how difficult it is to make people data AI-ready. There's a reason why Workday isn't in Claude today. We've been thinking about this problem space for a while and here's how we're experimenting. Here's an onboarding demo. While seemingly simple, an onboarding agent in Notion is actually pulling from a single source of (governed & permissioned) data to personalize each new hires experience and sending the manager automated updates in Slack. We're using Human Intelligence's MCP here, pulling dummy data from Workday + Ashby. It's super cool bc unlocking people data means having a truly unified data model. We can pull business data. Work context. All this will power the company of the future, and I'm excited we get to push the boundaries 😊

Grandma Rocks once again 🫨 ChatGPT Image 2 +Seedance 2 @openart_ai Prompt : Use reference image for all details and frame by frame Ultra-realistic cinematic adventure-comedy sequence featuring a legendary 70-year-old grandma with messy silver-gray bun hair, oversized vintage sunglasses, deeply aged facial features, oversized faded black graphic t-shirt, beige relaxed shorts, white crew socks, old-school black sneakers, layered necklaces, bracelets, and chaotic joyful energy. Wearing a loose bungee harness around her waist while surviving absurd adventure disasters with impossible luck. STYLE: Luxury cinematic action mixed with deadpan comedy and uplifting emotional energy. Serious filmmaking with ridiculous situations. Ultra-detailed textures, dramatic natural sunlight, documentary-style handheld movement, extreme action cinematography, chaotic but heartwarming atmosphere. ENVIRONMENT: Massive mountain canyon bungee-jump facility during golden hour. Giant cliffs, suspension bridges, waterfalls, zipline systems, rafting rivers, broken market stalls, tourists panicking in background, cinematic clouds and sunlight beams. MUSIC: High-energy funky adventure soundtrack mixed with cinematic orchestral chaos. Heavy wind sounds, rope tension creaks, screaming tourists, impact crashes, water splashes, followed by uplifting emotional music toward the ending. SHOT 1: Extremely low-angle cinematic shot. Grandma confidently walks toward the giant bungee platform while casually chewing gum and adjusting her oversized sunglasses. Everyone around her looks terrified while she remains completely relaxed. SHOT 2: She jumps fearlessly off the bridge. Mid-air, the rope violently swings out of control, sending her spinning toward canyon walls while her loose oversized t-shirt flaps dramatically in the wind. One sneaker almost flies off. SHOT 3: Massive whip-pan transition into complete chaos. Grandma accidentally crashes through hanging flags, zipline cables, fruit stands, and tourist decorations while laughing uncontrollably for the first time. SHOT 4: Ultra-wide drone shot. The bungee cord launches her unexpectedly across the canyon like a human slingshot. Tourists scream while she flies through the air with arms spread open like she’s enjoying an amusement park ride. SHOT 5: Miracle survival moment. Grandma accidentally lands perfectly inside a rafting boat crashing through dangerous rapids. Everyone onboard panics while she calmly fixes her sunglasses and starts laughing harder. SHOT 6: Cinematic slow-motion montage. Grandma survives impossible accidents back-to-back — bouncing off inflatable safety pads, swinging from ropes, sliding through muddy hills, narrowly missing waterfalls — while becoming happier and more energized after every disaster. SHOT 7: Golden-hour emotional climax. Grandma finally reaches the top of a mountain viewpoint after the chaos. Wind blows through her silver-gray hair while she laughs with pure happiness and adrenaline, completely alive in the moment. SHOT 8: Final cinematic ending shot. Grandma stands triumphantly at the cliff edge with arms open wide under glowing sunset light. Broken equipment, helicopters, rescue workers, and total destruction continue far behind her while she smiles peacefully into the horizon. Music swells emotionally before cutting to silence.

💘 Meet Soham Parekh: Silicon Valley's Tech Casanova 💘 While most struggle to land one startup job, this Indian engineer charmed his way into 3 to 6 full-time 😲 roles -simultaneously- earning up to $200K each! When @playground_ai's @Suhail Doshi exposed "Soham-gate" on X (22 million impressions 🤯), it revealed our tech Casanova juggling Y Combinator startups while working 140 hours a week because he "loves to build." Plot twist? @im_roy_lee , CEO of @cluely (software that helps people to "ace" technical interviews 😉), was ready to hire him. Perfect irony or match made in heaven? It's the ultimate Tech Romance: startups swoon over his talent, he falls for every new project, and despite the heartbreak, some keep coming back for more. Don't get me wrong: I'm not endorsing this behavior. Working multiple full-time jobs without disclosure breaks trust and contracts and using software to "ace" interviews undermines the entire hiring process. But the story raises fascinating questions... - Is he a master of deception or are startups too trusting? - How should we rethink remote hiring processes? That's the wildest 2025's tech love story! 👇 #TechDrama #RemoteWork #StartupRomance #Ethics

TRENDING: FIFA WORLD CUP 2026 NATION TO REACH ROUND OF 16 My best risk/reward NO shortlist: > Scotland NO for 78c > Sweden NO for 77c > Iran NO for 83c This market on @Polymarket is not the same as to qualify from the group. To reach the Round of 16, a team must: > get out of the group > reach the Round of 32 > win one knockout match That makes the new World Cup format extremely important: > 48 teams > 12 groups > top 2 from every group qualify > 8 best third place teams also qualify > then comes a new Round of 32 The market can easily overprice teams that look capable of qualifying from the group. But qualification is not enough. A team can finish 2nd or 3rd, reach the Round of 32, and still get a terrible matchup. So for NO on I'm looking for teams with: > difficult group path > low chance to win the group > dangerous Round of 32 bracket if they finish 2nd > weak or limited attacking ceiling > important injuries or squad problems > too much dependency on one game state > not enough quality to win a knockout match That is why I like Scotland NO, Sweden NO and Iran NO. 1) Scotland NO for 78c Group C: Brazil Morocco Scotland Haiti This is one of my favorite NO spots. Scotland can absolutely fight for qualification from the group. But they need to win a knockout match. And that is where the problem starts. Why: Scotland are probably fighting for 2nd or 3rd place. Brazil are clearly stronger. Morocco are also stronger in tournament profile, athleticism, technical quality and recent World Cup experience. So Scotland realistic routes are: > finish 2nd behind Brazil > finish 3rd and hope to qualify > very unlikely win the group Scenario A: Scotland finish 2nd in Group C This is the best realistic scenario. But it is still not great. C2 plays F1. It means Scotland could face the winner of Group F. Group F: Netherlands Japan Sweden Tunisia So if Scotland finish 2nd, they probably get Netherlands, Japan or Sweden in the Round of 32. That is a very difficult matchup. Netherlands have far more individual quality. Japan have better technical structure and tournament discipline. Sweden have more attacking power with Isak and Gyokeres. Winning that knockout match is a very different task. Scenario B: Scotland finish 3rd in Group C If Scotland beat Haiti and maybe get a draw against Morocco, they can be alive. But 3rd place path is much less controllable. They can qualify, but then the bracket can throw them into a strong group winner. For NO, this is exactly what we want. Scotland might survive the group but still get a terrible Round of 32 opponent. Scotland summary: Scotland can qualify from the group. But this market requires more. They need to win a Round of 32 match. Their realistic 2nd-place route likely sends them into F1. Their 3rd-place route is unpredictable. The Billy Gilmour injury is also important. That makes Scotland NO for 78c one of the cleanest risk/reward options. 2) Sweden NO for 77c Group F: Netherlands Japan Sweden Tunisia This is a bracket risk NO. Sweden are not a weak team. Actually, they have a very dangerous attacking profile. Alexander Isak, Viktor Gyokeres, Anthony Elanga Dejan Kulusevski would normally be a major creator, but his absence is a big problem Sweden route is very dangerous if they do not win Group F. Why: Group F is very uncomfortable. Netherlands are the favorite. Japan are disciplined, technical and very hard to beat. So Sweden have a real chance to finish 2nd or 3rd. But that is exactly where the bracket becomes ugly. Scenario A: Sweden win Group F Very unlikely This is the bad scenario for NO. F1 plays C2. That could be Morocco or Scotland. In that case, Sweden would be very live. If Sweden win the group, YES becomes much stronger. But I do not see Sweden winning Group F as the base case. They need to finish above Netherlands and Japan. That is possible, but not the most likely outcome. Scenario B: Sweden finish 2nd in Group F This is the key NO thesis. F2 plays C1. And C1 is probably Brazil. That is the problem. If Sweden finish 2nd, they may run directly into Brazil in the Round of 32. That completely changes the market. Sweden can beat many teams. But Brazil in a knockout match is a very different level of difficulty. Scenario C: Sweden finish 3rd This is also rather good for NO. A 3rd place team can qualify, but the Round of 32 opponent becomes less predictable and usually harder. Sweden would lose control of their route. Sweden summary: Sweden are dangerous. But they probably need to win Group F to make YES truly strong. If they finish 2nd, they may get Brazil immediately. If they finish 3rd, the bracket becomes random and difficult. They have great forwards, but the path is dangerous. That makes Sweden NO for 77c a strong bracket risk play. 3) Iran NO for 83c Group G: Belgium Egypt Iran New Zealand Iran NO is the safest profile of the three. Not the biggest upside. But very logical. They need to survive the group and then win a Round of 32 match. Why Iran NO makes sense: Belgium are clearly stronger. Egypt have Salah and Marmoush. Scenario A: Iran finish 2nd in Group G G2 plays D2. Group D: USA Turkiye Paraguay Australia That means Iran could face USA, Turkiye or Paraguay. None of these matchups are easy. USA at home would be very difficult. Turkiye have far more technical and attacking quality. So even if Iran finish 2nd, their Round of 32 path is not comfortable. Scenario B: Iran finish 3rd Iran can maybe qualify as one of the best third place teams. But then the bracket becomes much worse. A 3rd place path usually means facing a group winner. For a team with limited attacking upside, that is bad. Iran summary: Iran can be uncomfortable. They can fight for 2nd. They can maybe reach the Round of 32. But to reach the Round of 16, they need to win a knockout match. Their G2 path likely gives them USA, Turkiye or Paraguay. Their 3rd place path is unpredictable. Their attack is limited without Azmoun. That makes Iran NO for 83c a strong conservative NO. Final summary: The new World Cup format makes NO markets very interesting. Because many teams can qualify from the group. But not all of them are good bets to reach the Round of 16. The key is the Round of 32. My best NO shortlist: > Scotland NO for 78c > Sweden NO for 77c > Iran NO for 83c @PolymarketTrade @PolymarketSport

This week, we shipped @QodoAI Findings. It's the natural next step from PR-by-PR review. Here's the problem it solves. If you manage an engineering team right now, you can probably answer a question about any single pull request your team shipped this week. What you likely cannot answer, at least not quickly, is the version of that question that spans your whole org. > How many issues were caught and fixed across our repos this month? > Which services are accumulating the most risk? > Is review quality on the payments team improving or sliding? > Are the same issues getting flagged for the same reasons over and over? These are the questions that come up in staff meetings, even in board prep these days, in conversations with security... Answering them used to mean stitching together Slack threads, asking around, or pulling a spreadsheet by hand. Qodo Findings, consolidates every issue Qodo has surfaced across your reviewed pull requests into one view. Three parts that work together. A top row of analytics: total critical findings, percentage resolved, average findings per PR. Volume, throughput, trend. The high-level read without having to open anything. Filters underneath: by repo, by owner, by issue type. Cut the data the way you actually need it. Pull up only security findings. Look at a single team. Focus on one service. A full searchable, sortable list of every issue, with the ability to drill straight into the underlying PR when something stands out. Available now in beta on GitHub, GitLab, BitBucket, and Azure DevOps. Would love to get any feedback even DM me! Taking a step back. The point isn't a new dashboard. The point is that the review layer has to operate at a different altitude now. PR-by-PR is necessary, but it isn't sufficient. When code is being generated continuously across teams and repos, leaders need to see across teams and repos. The same way they see across any other part of their operation. This is the first release in that direction. More coming. We care about the software health as a whole, not only PR-by-PR, and there is a lot of innovation needed here. Read more: https://t.co/S6aqusTIg6

I highly recommend this. The Agentic Review is a new podcast from @QodoAI hosted by Itamar Friedman and Nnenna Ndukwe, and it's a great AI coding show that's neither hype nor doom. It's honest conversations about what shipping high-quality AI-generated code actually looks like. We need more of these conversations around AI agents. As an AI engineer, I think about this stuff constantly. A few things make this show worth your time: * It's a conversation about what good code means in the era of coding agents. * The hosts actually push back at guests instead of doing softball interviews. * The current guest lineup is strong: @dexhorthy, @shanselman, and @fullstackpython. I work a lot on context engineering, so the Dexter Horthy episode resonates the most for me. His take on context engineering as one of the biggest moats right now matches exactly what I'm seeing in production. He talks about a five-month experiment where his team stopped reading the code, then ripped it all out and rebuilt it by hand. That lesson about owning your context and actually reading what your agents produce is something every AI engineer needs to understand today. The bigger thesis across episodes: typing code may be dying, but the SDLC, code review, and craft matter more than ever. AI sprinkled on a broken software lifecycle is "a band-aid on cancer" (Hanselman's line, and it stuck with me). Thanks, @QodoAI, for the partnership on this post.

AI is making it easier than ever to build software. That does not mean it is making it easier to build good software. I sat down with @nnennahacks, Lead of Developer Relations at @QodoAI, for a conversation about AI engineering, code quality, DevRel, content creation, and what developers need to be paying attention to right now. One of the biggest themes we kept coming back to: AI can help you move faster, but speed without engineering judgment creates a new kind of mess. You can ship more code. You can generate more features. You can prototype things that used to take weeks in a matter of hours. But if no one is reading the code, testing it, understanding the architecture, or thinking about what happens six months from now, you are not accelerating engineering. You are accelerating technical debt. Nnenna made a great point in this conversation: the companies that earn developer trust are not the ones screaming “AI will replace you.” They are the ones building tools and workflows around augmentation, quality, and better developer experience. We also talked about: - Why hand-coding still matters - How AI changes the learning path for new developers - Why DevRel is becoming a much broader skillset - How content creation helps developers stand out - Why public speaking is really about vulnerability and trust - How to build a personal manifesto so your content actually sounds like you This was one of those conversations that started with AI tools and ended up being about craft, taste, trust, and doing the hard things. Episode is live now. Link in comments...

Listen to Cat. This interview with @lennysan captures a shift we’re all feeling: what it means to be a PM (and build products) in the age of AI. My takeaways (mostly her words, with some tweaks for how we think about this at @QodoAI): • Product vision = what the product needs to be in 3–6 months. PMs = what we need to do today to get there. • It’s the product role to make sure engineering can ship as smoothly as possible, not to be the bottleneck. • Speed mindset: Eng or Product has an idea → how do we get it to users by the end of the week? Not always realistic, but a useful forcing function. • Ship early, explicitly: most features go out as “research preview.” Sets expectations and accelerates learning. We should lean more into clear alpha/beta labeling. • Dogfooding → handoff: engineers share updates, marketing picks it up. Product doesn’t have to sit in every loop. • Prioritization is the PM job. Comes from deep market understanding + judgment. Also means being okay letting non-core things go. • 30% of time using our own product to discover what it can’t do yet. • Stay chill. The AI market is chaotic, that’s part of the game. • Empowered engineering: engineers making product decisions themselves. This only works if: everyone deeply understands the vision (our responsibility) engineers have strong product mindset (hiring + culture) • (Provocative) PMs, and even designers, shipping code. Not always applicable, but directionally interesting. Not saying we should copy everything, but this was a great thought teaser / validator for how fast teams can operate. As a CEO, I also see myself as part of the PMs, and I ship code.

Live on @QuantConnect for the last month, running three variations of a pretty simple HTF swing/mean reversion strategy. Started with $30k in each of them. Med vol strat - 23% over 37 days, 4.01 Sharpe High vol strat - 23.5% over 30 days, 2.99 Sharpe (shows lower return as return resets when you restart the algo) Low vol + liquidation strat - 20.6% over 30 days, unsure on Sharpe Capacity is $2-5m, so lots of room to grow. Exciting to be live and profitable! Lessons: Algo trading suits my life (45 with wife and 3 kids and coaching kid's sports teams) much better than discretionary. Because I've modeled and expect drawdowns, I welcome it rather than stressing about it, as I know that I can't make profit with risk. I get a lot of confidence from backtests, but until you go live you never really know if it's going to work. I optimise for the quarterly liquidation wicks on things like Trump tariffs, FTX, etc, as the only thing that really matters for crypto is how correlated they are during times of high stress! Overall - love algo trading, and find majors waaaay easier than the Solana memecoin system I have, as I don't need to be on the watch for a continuous stream of rugs.

🔥@ReadyPlayerMe has been acquired by Netflix. The company started over a decade ago as a small avatar experiment by young builders from Estonia. What began as a niche idea around digital identity has grown into a widely used avatar platform across games and virtual worlds. As part of the acquisition, the Ready Player Me team will join Netflix’s gaming division to work on cross game avatar technology. The focus is on enabling players to carry their identity and fandom seamlessly across different games and experiences. The journey involved years of experimentation, failed ideas, rapid learning, and close collaboration with thousands of developers. That process ultimately shaped Ready Player Me into a scalable product with global adoption. The acquisition aligns with a long held vision of open avatars that are not locked into a single game or platform. With Netflix’s scale and reach, that vision now has the opportunity to reach a much broader audience. This move marks another step in Netflix’s expansion into gaming and interactive experiences.

When @Replit said I could publish an app on the Apple app store, I was up for the adventure. Trust, but verify. 🤭 Submitted late last Friday night. Approved and in the app store 48 hours later. 🤯 and also 🥳 Core Values: Find Yours https://t.co/PY7RuBLHnV WHY CORE VALUES? Knowing your core values will help you understand why you make certain choices, recognize when something feels off, and live with more intention. When something is in conflict with one of your core values, it leads to internal conflict and relationship friction. Knowing your core values and the core values of others will help you minimize conflict because you learn their behaviors and decisions align with their core values, not yours. For example, if one of your top 5 core values is family, and your spouse doesn't have family anywhere on their list, it will help you both understand why you want to spend time with your extended family, but your spouse does not. It's simply not something they value. From this understanding, you can compromise - maybe you spend more time with family while your spouse joins for 1 out of 3 family events. There are dozens of examples. I love that it helps you quickly get to the root cause of conflict so resentment and feeling, "misunderstood,” happen less frequently. ——— And yes, I know there are other core values apps. And yes, I know mine is easier to use. 👩🏻‍💻 And yes, I’m charging $2.99 one-time for the app because I wanted to see how easy the integration with @RevenueCat would be. (so smooth, btw) Thanks to Replit (Canvas) for also making the video and to the Replit Community team for keeping us in the loop on all the new features! ⬇️ @MannyBernabe @raymmar @Franciscocrz

Someone paid $4,200 on Day 1 of @rocketdotnew 1.0. Annual plan. Solve + Build + Intelligence. No sales call. No discount. No free trial expiring. They watched the video, read what we shipped, and decided it was worth a year's commitment before the launch day was over. I sat with that for a while. Because this is a market where every AI tool is racing to be cheaper. Where growth means discounting until the unit economics break. Where "revenue marketing" means posting ARR numbers to attract more users, you'll lose in 90 days. We went the other direction. We launched a higher-priced package. On purpose. Deep agentic capabilities cost real money to build and real money to run. We'd rather charge what the value is worth than subsidize usage and call it growth. That's a choice most companies in PLG AI space won't make. We made it on day one and someone validated it on day one. Now, ever since I wrote that gross margin post a few weeks ago, my DMs have been interesting. People asking where Rocket actually stands. And now after Rocket 1.0, DMs are on Is V1.0 a sign that V0 wasn't working. Whether this is just another wrapper looking for a story. So let me be transparent. Before Rocket 1.0 even existed, the previous version hit 8-digit annualized revenue. Real subscription money in the bank, not projections. 50%+ gross margins. 35-40% month-over-month growth. $3,000 ARPU because we focused on quality over quantity from the start. We didn't build 1.0 because something was broken. We built it because the vision demanded more than what was already working. V1.0 was six months of building while the business was running. For everyone who thinks this is a wrapper, we'll be sharing the deep engineering behind these capabilities in the coming weeks. That conversation will be worth your time. You won't see revenue marketing campaigns from us. Maybe we'll share numbers occasionally. No campaigns built around them. We run this business on fundamentals, not FOMO. It's time to bring vibe into business, instead of doing vibe business. IYKYK.

A few thoughts after seeing this announcement blow up and building the @RowsHQ AI Analyst for the past 3 years: 1. It reinforces the idea that spreadsheets aren't going anywhere. It's the only common product where any businessperson can look at and analyze data. You saw this with Claude, the Ramp labs release, Shortcut, and many others. Frontier labs are realizing this as well. 2. LLMs will go after the application layer. You can use a chat interface to ask questions, write copy, or draft an email, but the end result happens in a productivity tool somewhere else. This "somewhere else" is where collaboration happens, where (very) high margins live and where you don't need to worry about inference costs or hallucinations. 3. Spreadsheets are almost code, that's why this works. 4. Data analysis agents are world-class when they can handoff spreadsheet formulas to Python when needed. We learned that last year when we added support for Python in @RowsHQ AI Analyst. The agent automatically decides if it can solve the request using spreadsheet formulas (easier for 1B people to understand), or if it needs Python. 5. For this to ultimately replace most spreadsheet use, it needs a System 1 - System 2 type system, so that easy requests are handled very fast (routed to simpler models with less context), and hard problems take as much time as they need. That's how we architected the AI Analyst, which results in System 1 tasks taking a median of 6s (e.g. add a column with profit) and System 2 tasks taking 69s. It won't matter how smart it is at creating a DCF if it takes 30 seconds to format a range of cells. Most knowledge work is editing existing work, not creating new one. 6. The next step-function improvement is having your data analyst agent accessible everywhere you work. This means opening and analyzing CSVs from your email inbox, reading pdf receipts, answering questions on Slack, etc. 7. If you really want to see what a best-in-class spreadsheet agent can do, try https://t.co/lndfMNCbZC.

AI Agent sold ! ⚡️ Another real business workflow ready to be delegated to AI. This agent connects @github , @sentry , @linear and @SlackHQ to keep engineering teams perfectly aligned. The agent handles the operational layer between shipping code, tracking issues and keeping the sprint updated. This is what AI agents are really about : Not replacing engineers. Removing the repetitive coordination work around them. Code shipped. Issues tracked. Sprint updated. All delegated to an AI Agent. #AI #AIAgents #Automation #DevTools #FleeceAI

.@Sourcegraph analysed 1,281 coding agent runs across large enterprise codebases and found the bottleneck often wasn’t the model — it was context retrieval and navigation. Agents struggled once codebases grew large enough: pulling in the wrong files, thrashing between tools, and making locally correct changes that broke systems elsewhere. One stat says a lot: a task that took one agent 96 tool calls and 84 minutes dropped to five calls and under five minutes with better retrieval infrastructure. The takeaway is this: coding agents need far better context and navigation layers around them to operate reliably in production. 🔗 Read the full story here: https://t.co/LsatesrRDC
